Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (13): Reading Input and Printing Output
Time for something a little more interactive. This is partly setup for the next chapter too — otherwise, honestly, I'm running low on material QQ.

Let's start with how JavaScript handles this. Usually, if you need a user to type something in and pass it to JavaScript for some calculation, you'd pair an HTML input element with a bit of JavaScript.

Now let's get back to Python. Python is a backend language, so the only thing we can interact with is the Terminal. So how do we use Python to talk to a user through the Terminal?
For that, we use a function called
. You can think of it like HTML's
element if that makes it easier to grasp.

is dead simple. The only catch is that you need a variable to receive whatever the user types, because this function is an expression and therefore returns a value (if you're not comfortable with expressions, I'd suggest
). On top of that, you can pass
a message to prompt the user with:
Once you've typed something and pressed Enter, the result gets stored in the
One extra thing worth mentioning: when you use
, Python actually pauses and waits right there, so any
after it won't show up yet:

print('Getting ready to summon a name.')
name = input('Enter your name: ')
print('This line only appears after the user finishes typing')
print(name)
print('Getting ready to summon a name.')
print('This line only appears after the user finishes typing')

One more aside: if you're using Python 2's
, there are a few extra details to keep in mind, because it changes type based on what the user enters. Python 2 also had another function called
, and the difference is that with
, any number, float, whatever the user types is always treated as a string. Just a small thing to be aware of.
got merged into one, keeping only
, and whatever the user types is always returned as a string.
In a lot of the earlier chapters we've been using the
function over and over, so by now you're probably pretty familiar with it.
But... why am I bringing it up again here? Because it's got some fun party tricks up its sleeve, which is why it's worth pulling out for another look.

parameter is another one you'll use a lot. Its default is
. What does that mean? In short, it's the newline character at the end of the output. If you change
and then run the following code, you'll notice the result looks different:

print('a', end='|')
print('b', end='|') # becomes a|b| on one line
print('b', end='|') # becomes a|b| on one line

Shaoxing eggs made with Shaoxing wine really are pretty tasty, but I'd suggest doing a bit of prep before making them. Grab a slice of dong quai (Angelica root) and a little goji berry, give them a quick simmer, then fish out the dong quai slice. Let the broth cool, add about 200cc of Shaoxing wine, drop the eggs into the broth, and refrigerate for a day. Done.

Time for something a little more interactive. We'll look at how you read input from a user in Python with input(), why everything comes back as a string, and then dig into print() — which has more going on than you'd think, including the handy sep and end parameters. It's also setup for the next chapter, because honestly I'm running low on material.
